i
Traditional cryptographic methods, such as symmetric and
X
rasymmetric encryption, have been the foundation of  secure
a
communication for a long time. They convert  readable data
into encoded formats decipherable only by key holders [4], [5].
However,  these methods often make encrypted data conspicu-
ous in digital communications, potentially attracting malicious
scrutiny due to the presence of sensitive information [6], [7].
This visibility can lead to targeted attacks aiming to crack
encryption or exploit implementation flaws [8].
Steganography presents a far more covert and sophisticated
alternative to cryptography. While encryption merely scramble
data by converting it into unreadable formats, it still signals the

presence of sensitive information, which can attract unwanted
attention. Steganography, on the other hand, eliminates this risk
by concealing the very existence of  the data. By embedding
messages within cover medium such as  images, audio files,
or text, steganography leverages the inherent redundancy and
subtle variations in digital media. This ensures that the carrier’s
appearance or behavior remains virtually unchanged, making the
hidden information imperceptible not only to casual observers
but also to sophisticated adversaries. This level of invisibility
offers a significant advantage over encryption, which may invite
attempts at decryption or tampering once identified [9], [10].
A. Related works
In modern steganography,  three basic principles  for con-
structing steganographic systems have been introduced. The
first, Steganography by Cover Modification (CMO),  requires
modifying a cover-medium and concealing a message within
the cover-medium. While effective,  this approach inevitably
introduces embedding changes  into the cover  image, which
could be detected through careful steganalysis  [11]–[14]. The
second principle is Steganography by Cover Selection (CSE),
which requires  the steganographer selecting a cover-medium
from an extensive database that can encode messages as a stego-
object. However,  this method typically has minimal payload
capacity, making it  impractical  for many applications where
larger amounts of data need to be hidden [15],  [16]. Finally,
Steganography by Cover Synthesis (CSY) involves creating a
stego-object containing secret messages from scratch. Although
this method was more of a theoretical construct rather than a
practical steganographic technique about a decade ago, advances
in digital  imaging and synthesis have started to make this a
viable approach [10], [17]–[19].
However, despite advancements in digital steganography, re-
cent steganalysis techniques such as [20] introduced an adaptive
statistical model that assigns dynamic weights to DCT channels,
prioritizing well-modelled ones, with variants for known and
unknown embedding channels. [21] presents TStegNet, a two-
stream CNN-based steganalyser combining a gradient subnet to
amplify confidence artifacts and an image subnet  to counter
adversarial noise.  [22] proposes a calibration-based method
that  identifies subtle statistical anomalies  in neural network-
based steganography overlooked by  traditional  steganalysis.
Consequently, conventional steganographic techniques are more
susceptible to detection and compromise as adversaries develop
advanced tools to uncover hidden data [23], [24].
Hence,  steganography has advanced through the develop-
ment of rigorous security models aimed at enhancing robust-
ness against adversaries. Early frameworks, such as the study
introduced by [25], utiliseed hypothesis  testing and relative

THIS MANUSCRIPT IS A PREPRINT UPLOADED TO ARXIV.
entropy to assess security but were limited by assumptions about
cover text distributions.

Addressing computational limitations,
[26] proposed a probabilistic game-based model emphasizing
conditional security, yet constructing provably secure schemes
remained challenging.
Further progress was made by [27], who formalized secu-
rity using computational  indistinguishability and introduced a
protocol based on rejection sampling,  though the requirement
of perfect oracle access constrained practicality. [28] explored
adaptive steganography using Syndrome-Trellis Codes, how-
ever, the complex model hinders implementation in real-world
scenarios.
Integrating cryptography,  [29] presented a dual-layer LSB
steganography model with symmetric encryption, effective for
images.  [30] introduced Adaptive Dynamic Grouping for lin-
guistic steganography, achieving near-perfect security,  though
challenges in generalization persist.
The growing importance of multichannel protocols is evident
in steganographic studies. In social media contexts, [31] pro-
posed a model using cover objects as pointers to an external
encrypted message via an out-of-band channel, but it remains
vulnerable to side-channel attacks like traffic analysis. Earlier,
[32] developed a multichannel system employing public sharing
platforms and encrypted storage to securely hide sensitive in-
formation, enhancing security but complicating synchronization
and key management.
Building on multichannel  communication,  [33]  and  [34]
combined multiple protocols and channels to distribute secret
data,  enhancing bandwidth and undetectability.  In quantum
steganography,  [35] developed a quantum secret sharing and
entanglement scheme for secure communication over multiple
quantum channels, while [36] extended this concept with Multi-
Channel Quantum Images (MCQI), using random embedding
in quantum video frames to enhance security. However,  these
works rely on specific network conditions, which limits their
adaptability and requires advanced infrastructure.
Advanced Steganography by Cover Synthesis  (CSY)  ad-
dresses  traditional  limitations  [10],  [25],  [37].  In text-based
CSY,  [38]  created cover  texts  to hide messages,  requiring
meticulous coordination. [39] employed RNNs to generate text
covers from secret bitstreams. In image steganography, [40] pro-
posed reversible texture synthesis. [19]’s SSS-GAN generates
stego images from secret messages via semantic labels.  [41]
developed an image steganography technique using guidance
features in synthesis to enhance security.
Despite these developments, many steganographic methods
rely on idealized assumptions, such as perfect knowledge of
cover text distributions or reliable communication channels and
access to perfect oracles. Computationally intensive techniques,
particularly those based on quantum mechanics, often prove
impractical in real-world settings. Furthermore, approaches such
as combine encryption and steganography which increases de-
tectability and unnecessary redundancy. Additionally, existing
models typically target passive adversaries and depend on a sin-
gle steganographic construction principle, limiting adaptability
and robustness in dynamic environments.
There is a clear need for more secure and resilient stegano-
graphic  techniques  that  effectively conceal  information and
withstand modern steganalysis, driving the exploration of in-
novative methods to ensure hidden data remains undetectable
even against advanced analytical techniques [24], [42], [43].

2
To address  these challenges,  a pioneering multi-channel
steganographic protocol was introduced in [44] to enhance the
security of SMS-based mobile banking transactions. Despite
its innovative approach, subsequent analyses identified critical
vulnerabilities, particularly to the Multi-Channel Man-in-the-
Middle Attack and the Multi-Channel Replay Attack. A subse-
quent study by [45] attempted to mitigate these issues, focusing
on the Multi-Channel Replay Attack, but significant weaknesses
persisted, especially against advanced multi-channel attacks that
continued to compromise confidentiality and integrity.
This study presents a security overview of the protocols in
[44] and [45], highlighting persistent gaps and emphasizing
the need for an enhanced approach against these multi-channel
attacks. Our proposed method builds upon previous attempts by
introducing novel approaches to strengthen security in adversar-
ial environments.
For the security analysis, an adversarial scenario is modelled,
focusing on the interaction between a probabilistic polynomial-
time adversary (A) and a challenger (Chal) representing the
protocol. This model quantifies the likelihood of an adversary
successfully compromising the protocol. By formalizing these
attack strategies, the protocol’s security is systematically eval-
uated.
For consistency and ease of  reference,  the notation used
throughout  this paper  is presented in Table I.

This notation
serves as the foundation for the forthcoming analysis of multi-
channel attacks within the structured framework of a stegano-
graphic game.
Game I.1. The game G comprises four critical phases:
A.  INITIALISATION: Chal selects m,m1,m2, computes b =
m ⊕ m1 ⊕ m2, and embeds b into s ∈ S using LSB
steganography.
B. CHALLENGE PHASE: Chal sends (m1,m2, s) over chan-
nels (C1, C2, C3) respectively.
C. ATTACK PHASE: Adversary A intercepts  (C1, C2, C3),
′
extracts b from s, and computes m = b ⊕m1 ⊕m2.
′
D. WINNING CONDITION: A wins if m = m, demonstrating
the protocol’s vulnerability.
The  probability  of A winning  the  game  is  defined  as
Prwin = Prextract · Printercept, where Prextract  is  the probability
of successfully extracting b and Printercept  is the probability of
intercepting m1 and m2. The advantage of A is then given by:

Proof. SECURITY ANALYSIS:
Because of known steganalysis techniques and the absence
of a key-based mechanism to protect b, the probability Pext of
extracting b is high. Since the adversary A can intercept  the
communication channels,  the probability Pint of  intercepting
m1  and m2  is also high. Both probabilities being greater
1
than  2λ make  the adversary’s advantage significant. Given
that b = m ⊕ m1 ⊕ m2 and A intercepts m1 and m2,  the
scheme is vulnerable to a multichannel attack compromising the
protocol’s confidentiality. Additionally, the lack of an adequate
integrity checksum makes  the protocol vulnerable to attacks
compromising its integrity. Although b is hidden within a cover
medium,  the probability of successful extraction depends on
factors such as the steganographic method used. Conventional.

Unlike traditional steganography by cover synthesis, which
involves creating a cover object such as an image, audio file,
text, or video that is directly influenced by the secret message,
the model proposed in this study takes a different approach.
It generates a non-suspicious cover object or parameter  that
contains no information about  the secret message. Moreover,
the cover object is not directly influenced by the secret message.
For example,  a simple weather update or a generic nature
photograph can serve as an innocuous cover object. This ensures

This section presents a description of the Hybrid Entropy-

Steganographic Protocol Phyb−stego = P Shyb  , which is built
upon the novel  steganographic system model of Section II.
The protocol  is motivated by scenarios where conventional
encryption fails  to provide a solution, and steganography is
adopted as an alternative approach to maintain confidentiality
and integrity.

A. Protocol Description
This protocol assumes that  the sender, Amara, and the re-
cipient, Ebere, share a secret value Vpri, generated via a secure
pseudorandom number generator (PRNG). Both individuals pos-
sess portable devices termed Autonomous Secure Transaction
Modules (ASTMs), which are initialized with Vpri. This shared
secret is employed to derive a steganographic key kstego.

5
gram outlines the secure embedding and transmission of a secret
ter (Pparams). It highlights key stages in the process,  including
secure and undetectable communication.
When Amara wishes to communicate confidential  informa-
tion m, she constructs two independent cover messages, m1
and m2, utilizing the methods delineated in Section II. These
messages are formulated to appear as benign content,  thereby
avoiding suspicion. The protocol comprises five fundamental
phases:
(A) Setup Phase: Amara begins the process by establishing
three distinct channels Ci ∈ C(1, 2, 3) to ensure secure
communication with the recipient. It is crucial to note that
although the channels Ci are needed for secure communi-
cation, individually, they are not secure. Amara generates
a unique nonces noncea, nonceb:
(B) Message Generation and Transmission:  Amara  com-
poses three messages (m1,m2,m) that she wants to send
to Ebere. The cover generation algorithm Synth takes the
secret value Vpri and the length ℓ of the secret message m as
inputs and outputs two cover messages (m1,m2), both of
length ℓ: (m1,m2) = Synth(Vpri, ℓ). Synth ensures that the
ℓ
generated cover messages (m1,m2) ∈ {0, 1} are pseudo-
random binary strings of  length ℓ. The cover-messages
(m1,m2) are designed to be unsuspicious and innocu-
ous and therefore mimics the distribution and entropy of
generic messages. She generates nonces (noncea, nonceb)
then transmits (noncea||m1, noncea||m2) to Ebere through
the communication channels (C1, C2).
(C) Message Masking and Encoding: To encode the secret
message m and ensure security, the secret value Vpri and
both cover messages (m1,m2) are utilized.  Initially, an
auxiliary P is generated by applying the Hashed Message
Authentication Code (HMAC) function to the concatena-
tion of Vpri and one of the cover messages mi. Next, Vpri
is XORed with P , and the result is hashed using a secure
hash function, resulting in a secret stego-key kstego.
The secret message m is  then masked along with m1,

Before proceeding with MAC verification, Ebere first checks
the freshness of the nonces to prevent replay attacks. If noncec
has already been used, it is rejected, and the decoding process
terminates by returning ⊥, indicating invalid result.
′
Ebere then calculates a new MAC using an HMAC opera-
tion with the stego-object s and the nonce noncec as inputs,
alongside the shared secret Vpri. By comparing the received
′
MAC with the computed MAC , Ebere ensures the integrity
and freshness of the stego-object, verifying that it has not been
altered during transmission.
If  the verification succeeds, Ebere proceeds to extract  the
′
stego-object s using the reconstructed stego-key kstego to extract
theb. The original secret message m is then reconstructed using
the function:.

In the realm of cryptographic and steganographic protocol
analysis, the definition and exploration of adversary models are
pivotal for evaluating the security robustness of any protocol
[55], [58]–[62].
This section presents the security analysis of the MC −MitM
Attack on the protocol described in Section III. The security
analysis presented in this section is supported by the following:
1)  the security assumptions outlined in Section III-B,
2) a novel Adversary Model  in Section IV-A with unique
characteristics and objectives  that pose a  threat  to the
protocol’s security.
A. Adversary Model
cs, cm
In order to evaluate the security of the protocol Phyb−stego
described  in Section III,  this  subsection formally describes
a novel MC − ATTACKS adversary model. This model  en-
capsulates a scenario where an adversary, characterized as a
bounded probabilistic polynomial time (PPT) adversary A, ac-
tively intercepts communications. The adversary’s primary goal
is to compromise the integrity and confidentiality of messages
transmitted across various distinct  communication channels,
thereby posing a significant threat to the protocol’s security.
Definition IV.1. MULTICHANNEL ATTACK:
A Multichannel Attack (MC − ATTACKS) is characterized by
a PPT adversary A, strategically reconstructing,  intercepting
and potentially altering secret messages exchanged between
participants over multiple,  distinct  communication channels
(C1, C2, . . . , Cn) ∈ C.  In this attack, A aims to reconstruct
or manipulate a secret message m transmitted via a communi-
cs, cm
cation protocol Phyb−stego, where the interception occurs simul-
taneously across the involved channels,  thereby compromising
the protocol’s integrity and confidentiality.
The following game presents an interactive security exper-
iment  that captures the Multichannel Attack scenario and the
interactions between communicating entities.
Game IV.1. MULTICHANNEL ATTACK
This game models the interactions in MC − ATTACKS sce-
nario between a challenger, denoted as Chal, and a Probabilistic
Polynomial Time (PPT) adversary, denoted as A. The game
proceeds as follows:
A.  INITIALISATION PHASE:  Chal  establishes  all  neces-
sary steganographic parameters, communication channels
(C1, C2, C3)  and  stego-key drawn  from K using  the
(cid:0)  (cid:1)
protocol’s setup  Setup(λ)  and generation algorithms
Synth(Vpri).
B. TRANSMISSION PHASE: Chal  composes  the messages
(cid:0)  ′  (cid:1)
m ∈ M, (m1,m2) ∈ M ,  and s ∈ S  where m is
the intended secret message concealed within s. These
messages are published to A via C1, C2, and C3.

C. ADVERSARY PHASE: The adversary A is granted oracle
access where it establishes a Man-in-the-Middle posture on
all communication channels, i.e., C1, C2, and C3, enabling
interception of  all messages  transmitted  through  these
channels including the stego-object s.
D. ANALYSIS PHASE: A compiles the intercepted data from
channels C1, C2, and C3 and endeavours to decode the
captured messages  including stego-object  to reveal  the
secret message m.
E. RECONSTRUCTION PHASE: Based on intercepted data, A
′
constructs a hypothesis m in an effort to reconstruct the
original message m. Concurrently, A engages in active
interference with the communication channels to replay,
forge,  or alter messages,  testing the robustness of  the
system’s security measures.
A succeeds if the game evaluates the accuracy of the guess;
′
If m = m,  the game outputs 1,  thereby signifying protocol
′
failure. However, if m ̸= m, the game outputs 0, indicating the
protocol’s resilience against the attack. Therefore, the security
of the protocol is defined as follows.

(MC − ATTACKS-secure),  if  for all PPT adversary A,  the

The adversary’s capacity in Game IV.1 are substantial,  re-
flecting a high-risk scenario for  the steganographic protocol
while Definition IV.2 requires the steganographic protocol  to
maintain the security and confidentiality of m even when all
communication channels are compromised by A. If the protocol
can withstand such an attack, it demonstrates resilience against
one of the most challenging scenarios.
It is crucial to methodically prioritize and address the adver-
cs, cm
sary’s capabilities in the security evaluation of Phyb−stego.

This
structured approach ensures a comprehensive protection of the
protocol against various threats, each targeting two core security
objectives: confidentiality, and integrity.
CONFIDENTIALITY ANALYSIS:  In the context of mul-
tichannel  attacks, we first  examine  the Reconstruction
capacity, where A utilises (m1,m2, s) to reconstruct m.
This  test  evaluates  the protocol’s measures  to protect
message content and maintain confidentiality. Details of
this analysis are provided in Section IV-B.
INTEGRITY ANALYSIS: The ability to handle unautho-
rized retransmissions  is  tested through the Interception
and Replay attack across channels  (C1, C2, C3).  Such
replay attacks,  although not directly altering data,  can
significantly disrupt  system operations and compromise
availability. This capacity is further scrutinized under the
multichannel man-in-the-middle (MITM) attack scenario,

8
where A intercepts and modifies messages, posing a direct
threat to data integrity and authenticity. In the context of
forgery, A’s attempts to forge new messages represent a
severe security challenge. These attacks test the protocol’s
ability to authenticate messages and verify the legitimacy
of content and its source, ensuring robust defence mecha-
nisms are in place. These aspects are elaborated in Section
IV-C.
The security assessment begins by evaluating A’s potential
to obtain the stego-key, as detailed in Section IV-B. Securing
the stego-key is imperative, preventing A from undermining the
protocol’s defences against sophisticated multichannel attacks.
B. Confidentiality Analysis
cs, cm

is  crucial  for maintaining  the  secrecy of  the communica-

MC − ATTACK depends on the confidentiality of the stego-key
kstego and the adversary A’s inability to compromise message
confidentiality.
Confidentiality of Secret Stego-key: We begin by establishing
the security of the stego-key kstego, which is crucial for ensuring
that an adversary A will find it challenging to compromise the
protocol.

Message Confidentiality: With the security of kstego estab-

C.  Integrity Analysis
Building upon the confidentiality analysis in Section IV-B,
cs,,cm
this section evaluates  the resilience of  the Phyb−stego proto-
col,  focusing on integrity—a critical  security objective. The
protocol’s defense against multichannel attacks relies on its
robustness against specific threats such as multichannel replay
and man-in-the-middle attacks.
Multichannel Replay Attack: Initially, we analyze the security
cs, cm
of the Phyb−stego protocol against multichannel replay attacks.
In this attack, A intercepts the messages (m1,m2, s) and, after
it has been delivered, resends the same messages.
Claim 3. By the Perfect Secrecy of MACs assumption,  the
cs, cm
protocol Phyb−stego holds security against multichannel replay
attacks MC − R under the MC − ATTACK adversarial model.
Proof.

The adversarial model allows the adversary A to inter-
cept and attempt to replay messages across all three channels
(C1, C2, C3). Consider the following game:

THIS MANUSCRIPT IS A PREPRINT UPLOADED TO ARXIV.
A. ADVERSARY  PHASE:  The  adversary  A  intercepts
(noncea ∥ m1),  (nonceb ∥ m2), and (noncec, s,MAC)
sent  by  the  sender  over  the  channels  for  potential
multichannel replay.
B. VERIFICATION: The recipient verifies (MAC) and checks
nonces freshness. Stale or replayed nonces result  in the
entire messages being discarded as a replay attack.
SECURITY ARGUMENT:
Considering an attack on all three channels (C1, C2, C3),
the probability of a successful attack given an attempt by A
is denoted as Pr[MC − RA(λ) = 1|A], and the probability
of an unsuccessful  attack is Pr[MC − RA(λ) = 0|A].
Analysing Pr[MC−RA(λ) = 1|A], three independent events
are considered: E1 is the successful replay attack on C1,
E2  is the successful  replay attack on C2, and E3  is the
successful replay attack on C3. Here,  the interest  lies in
the simultaneous occurrence of the events E1, E2, and E3,
which can be denoted as (E1 ∩ E2 ∩ E3). Since these
events are regarded as dependent, the probability of these
occurrences:
Pr[E1 ∩ E2 ∩ E3] = Pr[E1] · Pr[E2|E1] · Pr[E3|E1 ∩ E2]
Where Pr[E2|E1] is the conditional probability of replaying
m2 after m1 is replayed. The notation the E1] · Pr[E3|E1 ∩
E2] be the conditional probability of replaying s success-
fully after m2 after m1 are replayed.
Assuming the protocol  functions as  intended, Pr[E1]  is
negligible because m1 is a cover-message designed to be
innocuously genuine with a nonce (noncea∥m1), and does
not leak any information about m. Secondly, Pr[E2|E1] is
negligible for the same reasons as Pr[E1].
Under the assumption of Perfect Secrecy of the MAC, the
probability Pr[E3 | E1 ∩E] is negligible. Acceptance of the
stego-object s as authentic hinges on fresh and consistent
nonces noncec  that pass verification checks enforced by
the MAC’s security properties. By including the freshly
generated nonce noncec within the MAC, any replayed
′
stego-object s over channel C3 with a reused nonce will
be detected and rejected. Therefore,  the probability of A
attacking successfully given an attack attempt is as follows:
Pr[MC−RA(λ) = 1|A] = Pr[E1] · Pr[E2|E1] · Pr[E3|E1 ∩ E2]
≤ negl(λ) · negl(λ) · negl(λ)
= negl(λ)
Therefore,  the advantage of A attacking C3 is expressed
as:
MC−REPLAY  (cid:12)  (cid:12)
Adv  cs, cm  (λ) = (cid:12)Pr[MC − RA,(λ) = 1](cid:12) ≤ negl(λ),
A,Phyb−stego  (cid:12)  (cid:12)
Multichannel man-in-the-middle Attack: With the protocol’s
resilience against Multichannel Replay Attacks now established,
we proceed to assess its security in the context of Multichan-
nel Man-in-the-Middle (MC-MitM) Attacks. In, this attack, A
intercepts the three messages and modifies them before it  is
delivered.
Claim 4.  Under  the Perfect  Secrecy  of MACs  and  the
MC − ATTACK adversarial model, an adversary A attempting
a Multichannel Man-in-the-Middle Attack (MC-MITM) across

10
three channels C1, C2, and C3 has a negligible advantage in
′
modifying the secret message m to m without knowledge kstego.
Proof.  Consider the simultaneous occurrence of dependent
events Pr[E1 ∩ E2 ∩ E3] = Pr[E1] · Pr[E2|E1] · Pr[E3|E1 ∩
E2] where A intercepts messages noncea∥m1 from C1,
nonceb∥m2 from C2, and s from C3, aiming to modify the
′
original message m to a new message m . The intercepted
messages are combined with nonces noncea and nonceb,
while s  is generated using s = Enc(b, kstego, o)  and
MAC = HMAC(s, Vpri).
′
Therefore for A to be successful in modifying m to m , A
′
needs to ensure that the modified message (m ⊕m1⊕m2⊕
kstego) remains valid under  the steganographic encoding
and MAC generation process. Although without kstego, the
′
adversary is forced to guess a corresponding kstego  that
matches kstego, and a correct MAC for the newly formed
message, which is computationally infeasible due to the
Perfect Secrecy of MACs.
The message authentication code MAC = HMAC(s, Vpri)
ensures that each MAC appears equally probable, thereby
enhancing the protocol’s security by preventing A from
associating a MAC with a specific message or session.
Additionally, without the secret key, A cannot differentiate
MACs generated from various messages or nonces. More-
over, as previously analyzed in Section IV-B, A cannot
feasibly obtain kstego,  and  the  likelihood of  forging a
valid MAC without the correct key is minimal. Therefore,
the combined probability of successfully modifying the

metadata.section_title: Evaluation of Protocol Metrics: Methodology and Results

text:
This section evaluates  the effectiveness and robustness of
the hybrid steganographic protocol in practical scenarios. The
analysis begins with an examination of entropy and linguistic
plausibility of cover messages to ensure they appear natural and
contextually plausible,  thus minimizing detection risks. Next,
data size, processing latency, and transmission time are assessed
to determine the protocol’s efficiency and suitability for real-
time applications. Finally, metrics such as Peak Signal-to-Noise
Ratio (PSNR), Structural Similarity Index Measure (SSIM),
and Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) are measured to confirm that
embedding minimally impacts the cover object, enabling secure
and covert communication in sensitive environments.
A. Specification of the Implementation
This section outlines the construction and performance eval-
cs, cm
uation of the hybrid steganographic protocol Phyb−stego. Python
played a pivotal  role  in implementing this protocol due  to
its extensive libraries for cryptography, networking, and data
processing. A Markov chain was developed based on a text
corpus to probabilistically guide word transitions,  facilitating
the generation of coherent sentences. Randomisation during this
process was governed by a shared secret key Vpri, enabling the
generation of cover messages by initialising their secure pseudo-
random number generators (PRNGs) with this key. To the best
of authors’ knowledge, it is a novel approach that leverages a
secret key in conjunction with Markov chain to generate cover
sentences. The ‘secrets‘ module was employed to generate Vpri
for creating natural-looking cover messages, while the ‘PIL‘

THIS MANUSCRIPT IS A PREPRINT UPLOADED TO ARXIV.
library enabled least  significant bit  (LSB)  steganography in
the spatial domain for embedding secrets within cover objects.
Cryptographic integrity was ensured through the use of ‘hashlib‘
and ‘hmac‘, which produced secure stego keys and HMACs.
cs, cm
The  simulation of  a distributed Phyb−stego  involved vir-
tual machines for the sender (operating Ubuntu 24.04.1 LTS)
and  the  receiver  (operating Linux Mint Vanessa 21) on a
system configured with an Intel(R) Core(TM)  i5-6400 CPU
at  2.70 GHz  and 24.0 GB of RAM. HTTP servers  and
the ‘requests‘ library enabled real-time message transmission.
The implementation details can be found on GitHub at  the
following link: GitHub Repository https://github.com/Omego-
Obinna/hybrid-multi-stego
B. Cover Message Analysis
This section outlines the process for generating cover mes-
sages m1,m2 using a first-order Markov chain to model word
transitions within a given corpus. The subsequent  analysis
evaluates the effectiveness of this approach. The Markov chain
is represented as ChainMarkov = {w1, w2, . . . , wn}, where wi
denotes a word, and the transition probabilities are defined by
Pr(wj | wi) = Tij,
with T serving as the transition matrix. A shared secret key
Vpri, created using HMAC during the initial setup,  is used to
seed the pseudorandom number generator (PRNG):
seed = FPRNG(Vpri)
This seeded PRNG ensures  that communicating entities can
generate identical sequences of pseudo-random numbers, which
regulate the word transitions in the Markov chain. The process
for generating a cover message is expressed as:
Pparams = FMarkov(FPRNG(Vpri)),
where Pparams = {w1, w2, . . . , wn} is the generated sequence
chain
of words. The function Markovbuild () prepares  the Markov
model by segmenting the corpus into sentences and constructing
the transition probabilities Pr(wj | wi). To ensure deterministic
text generation, the PRNG is seeded using a hash of Vpri:
seed = H(Vpri)
generate
The function Markovtext  () begins with an initial word
(randomly chosen or predefined) and selects subsequent words
based on the transition probabilities defined by T . This method
produces text  that appears natural and unsuspicious, allowing
the sender  to embed a secret message m within the cover
message Pparams, which the recipient can later recover.
Shannon Entropy [63],  [64] was used to measure the un-
predictability in cover messages. Higher entropy denotes more
randomness; lower entropy reflects predictability. The results in
Table II shows the entropy values ranges from 3.52 to 4.20,
indicating a moderate level of randomness in the messages. For
example,  the sentence ”hops quietly through the garden, nib-
bling on fresh clover” had the highest entropy (4.20), suggesting
a well-distributed word pattern akin to natural language. This
balance ensures that  the messages are neither overly random
nor predictable, making them indistinguishable from normal
communication [64].
Linguistic plausibility was evaluated using readability scores
and grammar checks.

showing cumulative  time progression  through  the protocol
phases, illustrating where significant time accumulations occur.
ranges from 71.82 to 116.15,  showed simpler messages like
”they run through the tall grass” scored higher, while complex
sentences scored lower. This aligns with everyday communi-
cation [65]. Grammar level scores ranged from 0 to 12, with
more formal structures scoring higher,  further enhancing the
messages’ authenticity.
Overall,  the combined analysis of  entropy and  linguistic
plausibility demonstrates that the cover messages generated by
the Markov chain model are unpredictable and resemble hu-
man communication. The moderate entropy values (3.52–4.20)
reflect a balance, while varied grammar and complexity enhance
their naturalness, reducing detection risk.
C. Analysis of Protocol Efficiency
Protocol Execution Times are detailed in Figures 3 and Table
III, covering key phases such as embedding, transmission, and
decoding. Execution times for each phase and the cumulative
time are analyzed,  focusing on metrics like transmission and
latency across stages.

inability of an observer to distinguish a cover object from its
corresponding stego object, which contains hidden information.
This property is assessed using metrics such as Peak Signal-to-
Noise Ratio (PSNR), Mean Squared Error (MSE), and Struc-
tural Similarity Index (SSIM) [66], [67], as shown in Figures 6
and 7.
MSE quantifies the average squared pixel differences between
the cover medium o and the stego object s. A near-zero MSE,
as shown in Figure 6, indicates minimal pixel-level deviations
caused by embedding the hidden data. This suggests that  the
embedded information introduces negligible changes, support-
ing the indistinguishability property [68]. When MSE ≈ 0, the
likelihood of a pixel mismatch, P(oi ̸= si),  is extremely low
for all pixels i, reinforcing the protocol’s effectiveness at  the
pixel level [66].
PSNR measures the ratio between the maximum power of the
signal (the cover object, or the image before embedding) and the
noise introduced (differences from embedding). A PSNR value
of 84.62 dB, as shown in Figure 6, reflects very high similarity
between the cover and stego images. In steganography, PSNR
values above 40 dB typically indicate that  the stego image
is visually indistinguishable from the cover  image [69]. The
high PSNR observed in the Figure underscores the minimal
perceptual differences introduced by the protocol.
SSIM evaluates the perceptual quality of images based on
luminance, contrast, and structure. As depicted in Figure 7, most

demonstrates the protocol’s ability to preserve image quality
and fidelity.

SSIM values are clustered near 1, with a global SSIM close to
1, as also highlighted in Figure 6. Values near 1 signify a high
structural  resemblance,  implying that  the embedding process
does not compromise the perceptual or structural integrity of the
image. This outcome further supports the indistinguishability
property.
From a probabilistic perspective,  the indistinguishability of
the stego image can be analyzed using conditional probabilities.

Given the observed high PSNR, low MSE, and near-unity SSIM

This section aims to demonstrate the protocol’s practicality
and effectiveness (Sections II and III) through case scenarios.
The first scenario (Section VI-A) examines SMS mobile bank-
ing in low-connectivity areas,  showcasing its confidentiality
under bandwidth constraints. The second scenario (Section
VI-B)  illustrates  the protocol’s role in enhancing privacy in
Blockchain, ensuring secure transactions and confidentiality in
resource-abundant settings.
A. Application to SMS Mobile Banking
In low-connectivity environments,  traditional banking faces
significant challenges, making SMS banking a vital alternative
where data access is limited or unreliable [70]–[72]. This is
particularly critical in rural areas, where SMS coverage has been
extended to support banking needs [73]–[75]. However, SMS
inherently lacks end-to-end encryption,  raising data security
concerns that necessitate robust security protocols [76]–[78].
The protocol introduced in Section III strengthens SMS security
by embedding sensitive data, enabling secure financial services
even in low-connectivity scenarios.
This  section explores a case study  inspired by Amara’s
attempt  to securely transfer funds to Ebere’s account amidst
technological and environmental constraints. Amara relies on
public cybercafe´s for online activities due to her lack of a private
internet  connection. These  settings  are  inherently  insecure,
exposing her to external hacking risks. Moreover, Amara’s dual-
SIM, low-powered mobile phone lacks internet capabilities and
does not  support mobile banking apps,  further  limiting her
options for secure transactions. Consequently, she is compelled
to depend on SMS communication.
Additionally, mobile network operators (MNOs) are often
perceived as untrustworthy due to their access to plaintext SMS
content, which raises concerns about potential misuse, including
selling sensitive information to third parties [79]–[83]. While
the bank is considered trustworthy, the involvement of MNOs
and cybercafe´s introduces significant security risks.

15
To mitigate these challenges, the study assumes the bank pro-
vides an Autonomous Rural Secure Transaction Module (AR-
STM), a portable,  low-powered device capable of performing
secure computations offline. This device allows secure banking
transactions without reliance on robust  internet  infrastructure,
addressing the outlined vulnerabilities.
cs, cm
The implementation of Phyb−stego in the SMS setting begins
with the secure setup of parameters using the ARSTM. During
the Setup(λ) phase, Amara uses the ARSTM to derive a private
key Vpri and a critical value P , which help generate the stego-
key kstego, following the approach in Section II. This stego-key
is essential for securely encoding sensitive financial messages.
In the Synth(Vpri, ℓ) phase, Amara generates pseudo-SMS
banking instructions (m1,m2). These messages resemble gen-
uine banking SMS formats, ensuring they appear inconspicuous.
To enable the steganographic transaction, Amara establishes
three distinct communication channels: C1, C2, and C3. Chan-
nels C1 and C2 are used to transmit m1 and m2 over mobile
networks.
For transmitting b through C3, Amara leverages the cybercafe´
environment. She can encode b within charts (o) displayed
on the bank’s webpages, taking advantage of their natural and
unsuspicious presence in financial contexts. For instance, a chart
illustrating interest rate trends, customer behaviour statistics, or
product performance metrics could encode (b) through slight
variations  in data points,  carefully chosen to remain within
plausible and contextually appropriate ranges. The transmission
occurs via standard cybercafe´ internet services, and to further
enhance privacy, HTTPS protocols. When feasible, a VPN could
be employed to obscure the data’s origin.
Upon extracting b, the bank verifies its integrity and authen-
′
ticity by computing its own (MAC ) and comparing it with the
transmitted (MAC). By seamlessly integrating steganographic
techniques into common cybercafe´ activities, C3 ensures the
confidentiality and integrity of Amara’s communications. The
steganographic process cleverly conceals  transactional  data,
making these messages indistinguishable from genuine banking
communications.
This technique enhances payment indistinguishability, a crit-
ical objective quantified as follows:

Here, mi  represents pseudo-SMS banking instructions, while
mgenuine refers to genuine instructions. This definition demon-
strates the protocol’s robustness in ensuring transaction con-
fidentiality. By leveraging kstego,  the protocol  also prevents
adversaries from executing successful multichannel man-in-the-
middle attacks,  as  they cannot decode  the original banking
instructions m. Consequently, financial  transactions are safe-
guarded against such threats.
B.

Application to Blockchain Technology
Blockchain technology, known for  its robust  security and
decentralized nature, has revolutionized data integrity and trust
in digital transactions [84]; including various applications [71],
[85], [86]. While blockchain inherently offers security through
its cryptographic mechanisms and distributed ledger technology,
the  transparency of  its  transactions can be a double-edged
sword. In many use cases, the visibility of transaction data on.

This study introduces a novel hybrid steganographic model
and communication protocol that advances secure communica-

16
tion by integrating Steganography by Cover Modification and
Steganography by Cover Synthesis. This approach enhances
both security and undetectability, making it suitable for highly
censored or adversarial environments.
The protocol’s application in multichannel communication
further strengthens its resilience against advanced threats like
Multichannel Replay and Man-in-the-Middle attacks. While
implementation within Blockchain environments may pose chal-
lenges  related  to computational  overhead and  transparency,
proper optimization can significantly enhance the security of
Blockchain-based financial services and other applications.
A. Feasibility
Compared  to other multichannel  protocols,  the proposed
hybrid model uniquely integrates Steganography by Cover Mod-
ification and Steganography by Cover Synthesis to achieve both
high security and undetectability. This protocol uses multichan-
nel communication not only to enhance security but also to
complicate detection, even if some channels are compromised,
by combining dynamic cover generation and integrity check-
sums. This ensures data remains secure and undetectable across
multiple channels.
The protocol upholds core steganographic security principles.
Even if channels are compromised, hidden message integrity
and confidentiality are protected by strong security techniques,
multi-layered channel security, and integrity checks. Publishing
the protocol’s design does not weaken security, as per ”security
by design” and Kerckhoffs’ principle, as long as secret keys
remain confidential.
Implementing  the  proposed  steganographic  protocol  in
Blockchain  is  feasible and promising due  to Blockchain’s
decentralized,  secure,  and transparent nature. Platforms  like
Ethereum and Hyperledger can handle the computational de-
mands of steganographic embedding with optimization. The
protocol’s dual-method design secures data concealment.
B. Limitations
This  research introduces an innovative approach however
there may be potential  limitations,  especially in Blockchain
environments. Combining steganography with integrity mech-
anism may add computational overhead, potentially reducing
transaction throughput, particularly in speed-focused systems
like financial services. Additionally,  integrating steganography
may challenge Blockchain’s transparency requirements, creating
a difficult balance between secrecy and visibility.
Finally, hashing algorithms,  like SHA-256 or SHA-3,  are
essential  for ensuring data  integrity,  as weaker options  like
MD5 are vulnerable to collision attacks. Despite limitations
like computational complexity and assumptions about adver-
sarial capabilities,  this model advances secure communication
systems. Future research can focus on optimisation, and large-
scale deployment to enhance real-world applicability.
